author: Aeschines
resource: Recognition as a citizen and social standing
speaker: Aeschines
verbatim: 2.152. the father of Philon and Epicrates, the man by whose good offices you were enrolled among the men of your deme, as the elder Paeanians know. Aeschines insinuates that only by some extraordinary favoritism could Demosthenes, with his strain of Scythian blood, ever have been recognized as an Athenian of pure blood, and so enrolled in the citizen-list when he came to manhood.

author: Andocides
criterion_label: Wealthy individuals can use their resources to influence legal outcomes
speaker: Andocides
verbatim: Thanks to his riches he can be sure of protection in plenty. None the less, when a man offers violence to his own wife and plots the death of his brother-in-law, how is he to be expected to behave towards such of his fellow-citizens as cross his path?

author: Andocides
criterion_label: State-debtors lose civic rights until debts are paid
speaker: Andocides
verbatim: First, state-debtors. All who had been condemned on their accounts when vacating a public office, all who had been condemned as judgement-debtors... had to pay within eight Prytanies; otherwise, the sum due was doubled and the delinquent’s property distrained upon.
